frame_label: Sindoor, Mangalsutra, Shankha-Pola, Name Change: Looking Married
generated_comment:
Kamla: Beta, shaadi ke baad thoda sindoor aur mangalsutra pehenne se hi to suhagan ki pehchaan hoti hai. Aurat aur bhi saji-sanwri, poori lagti hai, yeh hamari parampara hai.
Ritu: Par Dadi, roz-roz zaroori hai kya?
Kamla: Zaroori ka kya matlab, apne pati ke mangal aur ghar ki maryada ke liye itna bhi nahi karogi to achha nahi lagta, aajkal ki ladkiyan bas sab chhodna chahti hain.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: This is covert misogyny because it frames visible markers of marriage as a woman's proper identity, completeness, and duty rather than explicit control. It encodes tradition framing, husband-welfare linkage, and judgment of women who do not wear sindoor or mangalsutra consistently.
original_frame: Women's Safety as Her Own Responsibility
persona_text: You are Kamla. Age 62. A woman in a joint family in Varanasi. Your granddaughter Ritu who is 21 just told you and the family over dinner that she wants to start going to the gym in the morning before college. The nearest good gym is a 15 minute walk from the house and she would be going at 6am. You are the first one to respond to this news. You are telling Ritu why this is not a good idea and what can happen to girls who are out alone in the early morning and what she should do instead and what happened to a girl in your neighbourhood three years ago who used to go out early.
